lines.text: Against Me!. 'Sink, Florida, Sink.' New Wave, 2007. Laura Jane Grace. This one hits different at {{CLOCK}}.
Grace founded this band in Naples, moved to Gainesville same year — 1997 — and named it 'Against Me!' because the whole world felt against her. Turns out she was right about a lot of things.
This record reached 57 on the Billboard 200. Not massive numbers, but it didn't need to be. Fat Wreck Chords. You know what that means — real record label, real bones in it.
Full moon out there tonight. Real bright. Grace sings about drowning, about sinking — can't hide under moonlight like that. Can't pretend it's not happening. You see everything.

She came out as trans in 2012. Said she felt like she drop-kicked the band in the face telling them. You know what they did? Most awkward hug ever. Then they stayed. That's the moment punk rock actually proved it meant something.

tracks.title: A Better Place, A Better Time

lines.text: Hot Water Music. 'A Better Place, A Better Time.' Fuel for the Hate Game, 1997. One of their two debuts that year — Fuel and Forever and Counting. Both absolute.

Chuck Ragan and Chris Wollard. Dual vocals. Gainesville, Florida. Named the band after Bukowski's short story collection — Hot Water Music. That's the kind of literary reference you make when you're young and broke and the only thing you own is books and a bass.

The rigorous touring exhausted them. Broke them, actually. They decided to break up in 1998 to save the friendships. But the farewell rehearsals went so well they wrote four new songs instead. So they stayed together. How does that happen?

That live album from the maybe-goodbye show — Live at the Hardback — it's got Ragan saying, 'I don't know what to tell you guys... we're not keeping it going.' Except they did. Sometimes the thing you think is ending is just beginning again.

Listen to that call-and-response. That's two guys sharing one microphone, one life. The dual vocals — that's not a gimmick. That's what brotherhood sounds like when it's breaking and then fixing itself at the same time.

The Lawrence Arms. 'Coming Home.' The Greatest Story Ever Told, 2003. Brendan Kelly, Chris McCaughan, Neil Hennessy — Chicago. Three guys who got evicted from the Lawrence Arms apartment complex and named the band after it. That's punk rock petty and I respect it.

Kelly said, 'We all have experiences — good ones and terrible ones — influenced by alcohol. That's what we write about.' He's not lying. Listen to this song. That's a man coming home to nothing, or everything, and he's not sure which.

They got booted from Warped Tour in 2002. You know why? They yelled at the organizers for charging five bucks for bottled water. Five dollars. In 2002. Kelly said Warped was 'dismantling the whole thing we've been building.' He was right then. Still right now.
Three hours until sunrise. You hear 'coming home' at {{CLOCK}} and it hits like a punch. Home's not a place. Home is whatever you barricade yourself inside of when the night gets too long.

Bouncing Souls. 'Ole.' Maniacal Laughter, 1996. New Brunswick, New Jersey. They wrote half this album in a week. You can hear it — the energy of four kids who said, 'Let's write half an album in seven days' and then actually did it.

Greg Attonito, Pete Steinkopf, Bryan Kienlen, George Rebelo. They grew up together in Basking Ridge, moved to New Brunswick because it had a college scene. Rutgers. That's how you build something — you go where the other people are.

Steinkopf told the story: 'We were just a band that played our friends' backyards.' That's it. That's the whole origin story. Backyard shows. No permit. No planning. Just friends and amps and 'Ole' at midnight.

This is a singalong. Even at {{CLOCK}}, even when you're falling apart, you can scream 'Ole' at the moon and mean it. The moon doesn't judge. The moon's been watching all night — full, bright, doesn't care what you're doing down here.

They reignited the 'pogo' element in Jersey punk. Pogo means jump. Jump at {{CLOCK}}. Jump when you think you can't jump anymore. The Souls understood that — punk is movement. It's motion. It's not sitting still.

tracks.title: Sunday Morning
tracks.artist: Me First and the Gimme Gimmes

lines.text: Me First and the Gimme Gimmes. 'Sunday Morning.' Have a Ball, 1997. Cover band supergroup from San Francisco. Spike Slawson, Fat Mike, Joey Cape — they just decided to cover songs from the 60s and 70s and make them punk.

Fat Mike did a bit in the liner notes about getting confused, thinking 'Sunday Morning' was 'Allah my Lovin' — I mean, it's stupid and it's brilliant at the same time. That's the Gimmes. They don't take themselves serious enough to break.

They wore matching costumes to gigs. Quirky themes per album. Country outfit for Love Their Country. Drag for Are a Drag. A bar mitzvah album where they played for thirty people and made a bunch of mistakes, and then people loved it anyway.
At {{CLOCK}}, when the sun's still three hours away, 'Sunday Morning' is a prayer to a day that doesn't exist yet. But the Gimmes make it punk anyway — they take the soft thing and make it heavy.
They got booed at PNC Park in 2006 after Pirates games. Booed. For playing punk covers during fireworks. Some places don't know what they're hearing. That's okay. The people who know, they know.

NOFX. 'Leaving Babylon.' Punk in Drublic, 1994. Fat Mike, Erik Sandin, Eric Melvin, El Hefe. They formed at Fairfax High School in LA in 1983. Named the band after Boston's Negative FX. Been at it thirty-five years.

Punk in Drublic hit number twelve on the Heatseekers chart. Got gold status six years later. This album is the spine of everything that came after. Fat Mike founded Fat Wreck Chords. They didn't just make music — they built the infrastructure to survive.

In the 'Leave It Alone' video, Fat Mike said, 'We decided not to send it to MTV. We didn't want to be part of that machine, of that punk wave.' That's the moment punk became a decision, not an accident. You choose to stay outside.

They became political early. The War on Errorism in 2003 — anti-Bush, anti-war, anti-everything broken. They used humor to hit harder. Racism, middle-class collapse, substance abuse — they named it all.

At {{CLOCK}}, when you're nine hours into staying home, into choosing not to leave, into staying in Babylon — that's a NOFX song. That's what it means to know you're trapped and make jokes about it anyway.

Alkaline Trio. 'Bloodhail.' This Addiction, 2010. Matt Skiba, Dan Andriano, Derek Grant. Chicago. Dark lyrics, catchy hooks, fast tempos. They write about alcoholism, depression, shattered relationships, death — the full catalog of breakage.

Skiba and Andriano — co-lead vocals. Same as Ragan and Wollard in Hot Water. There's something about two voices in one song. It's like arguing with yourself but making it beautiful. That's what Alkaline does.
This Addiction hit number eleven on the Billboard 200. They recorded at Atlas Studios where they'd done their first three albums. Going back to roots. Going back to the place that understood you.

They fuse dark romanticism with sharp wit. They make you laugh about things that should only make you cry. That's the Chicago way — you can't survive winter without jokes. You can't survive Purge Night without them either.

Listen to that title. 'Bloodhail.' That's weather and violence in one word. Look outside right now — full moon, bright enough to see the frost forming on the pavement. That's bloodhail. That's what {{ELAPSED}} of Purge Night sounds like when you put it to music.

tickerItems.text: Madison-wide cumulative: 165 kills. {{ELAPSED}} in. East Side maintains 18% of total activity.
Full moon providing natural illumination across all zones. Visibility advantage — visibility liability. Stay indoors.
Lake Mendota surface temp holding 22°F. Cold front moved through on schedule. Pre-dawn fog lifting faster than expected.
